<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm this zone is correctly isolated — test code imports nothing from production packages at the community-detection level.
- The breadth of coverage (architecture policy, delegation, orchestration, sync, dev server) signals mature end-to-end confidence in the CLI contract; keep these tests as the canonical regression gate for any cli.js changes.
- With 14+ files all at the top-level tests/e2e/, consider grouping by domain (e.g. tests/e2e/cli/, tests/e2e/policy/) if the suite continues to grow to make triage faster.
- Zero coupling to production zones is the correct property for an e2e suite — tests invoke the CLI as a black box rather than importing internals.
- Architecture-policy test living alongside CLI tests is a good pattern; ensures structural constraints are verified in the same CI pass as behavioral ones.
- The cli-e2e-tests zone treats the CLI as a black-box contract boundary — zero imports from production zones means any internal refactor is invisible to the test suite, which is the correct property for an integration gate.
- cli-e2e-tests is the only zone in the entire codebase that achieves both perfect cohesion (1.0) and perfect isolation (coupling 0) at scale (14 files). It should be used as the reference model when onboarding new test suites — any future e2e suite that imports from production zones should be treated as a structural defect rather than a convenience.
- The architecture-policy test co-located with CLI behavioral tests means a single CI job validates both structural constraints and behavioral contracts simultaneously. This is an efficient guard against regressions in either dimension but couples the policy enforcement cadence to the CLI release cadence — if CLI tests become slow, policy checks slow down too. Consider whether policy tests warrant a separate, faster CI step as the suite grows.
- All 14 test files in cli-e2e-tests use the .js extension while every package-level test (unit, integration) uses .ts. This cross-tier language inconsistency is an implicit convention (e2e tests as black-box shell scripts vs typed unit tests) that is nowhere documented — future contributors will choose the wrong extension without guidance.
- architecture-policy.test.js enforces structural code constraints, not CLI behavior, yet it is named and co-located identically to cli-*.test.js behavioral tests. A contributor triaging a failing suite cannot distinguish a policy violation from a behavioral regression without reading the file content.
- Rename architecture-policy.test.js to policy-architecture.test.js or relocate it to tests/policy/ to visually distinguish structural enforcement from CLI behavioral tests. Co-location with cli-*.test.js creates a false equivalence between two categorically different test concerns.
- Document the implicit convention that e2e tests use .js (black-box CLI invocation) while package tests use .ts (typed unit/integration). Add a comment in tests/e2e/ README or enforce via the architecture-policy test itself to prevent future contributors from adding .ts files to the e2e suite.
- The architecture-policy test runs inside the CLI e2e suite, coupling policy enforcement cadence and reliability to CLI build availability. Extract structural policy tests into a standalone tests/policy/ suite that runs without spawning CLI processes — this makes policy checks fast enough to run as a pre-commit hook independently of e2e infrastructure.
- [call graph] 367 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
